blurb: Rich, caramelised beef slow-cooked in coconut milk until the sauce intensifies into a dark, fragrant coating.

prov.popular_in: West Sumatra, Indonesia; Padang restaurants worldwide
prov.famous_for: CNN's World's Best Foods #1 (2011, 2017); culinary icon of Minangkabau cuisine
prov.consists_of: Beef braised in spiced coconut milk until liquid evaporates and spices caramelize into dark coating
prov.name_origin: Minangkabau language; 'rendang' refers to the slow-cooking process
prov.why_loved: Deep complexity from slow reduction; tender beef with intense coconut-spice caramelisation
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — constant stirring over low heat until coconut milk reduces and caramelises

brief.lead: Beef rendang is Indonesia's most celebrated dish — tender chunks of beef slow-braised in coconut milk with aromatics until the sauce reduces to a dark, caramelised coating. The dish evolves through distinct stages: gulai (soupy curry), kalio (thick curry), and finally dry rendang where the liquid has nearly evaporated and the meat turns rich brown.
expect: Tender beef coated in dark caramelised spice paste.
The long reduction creates impossibly tender meat with concentrated flavour.
Coconut milk reduction intensifies flavours.
Four hours of gentle cooking transforms the liquid into a rich, fragrant coating.
Aromatic spice base with galangal, lemongrass, and kaffir lime.
Traditional Minang aromatics provide the distinctive Indonesian flavour profile.
Served with steamed rice.
The intense, rich flavour needs plain rice to balance it.

ing: beef top round or topside, cut into 4 cm pieces
tl: Blend spice paste, assemble cooking components
Combine beef with spice paste and coconut milk, bring to boil
Maintain gentle simmer, stir frequently until liquid reduces
Continue stirring as mixture darkens and coating forms
Allow flavours to settle before serving over steamed rice
steps: Prepare the spice paste
Blend chillies, shallots, garlic, galangal, and ginger into a smooth paste. Bruise lemongrass stalks and knot them. Set aside.
WHY: Creating a smooth aromatic paste ensures even flavour distribution and proper caramelisation during cooking.
Start the braising process

In a large non-stick pan, combine beef pieces with the spice paste. Add tamarind, salt, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and turmeric leaves if using. Pour in the coconut milk and mix everything together.
WHY: Combining all components at the start allows the beef to absorb flavours as the coconut milk slowly reduces.
Initial simmer
Stir constantly over medium heat until the mixture comes to a boil. Reduce heat to low and continue cooking, maintaining gentle simmer.
WHY: Constant stirring during the first hour prevents sticking and ensures even cooking of the beef.
Slow reduction phase
Continue cooking over low heat, stirring occasionally. The coconut milk will gradually reduce, and the sauce will thicken and darken.
WHY: This lengthy reduction phase is where the complex flavours develop and intensify.
Final caramelisation
Increase stirring frequency as the liquid evaporates. Continue until the beef is tender and coated in a moist, dark brown paste.
WHY: The final stage creates the signature rendang coating — caramelised spices clinging to tender beef.
Rest and serve
Remove from heat and allow to rest for 10 minutes. Transfer to a serving dish and serve with steamed rice.
WHY: Resting allows flavours to settle and the coating to set slightly for better texture.
pair: Neutral rice balances the intense, rich flavours of rendang
Sweet tea provides refreshing contrast to the rich, spiced beef

blurb: Spiced beef mince baked with a golden egg custard topping — South Africa's national dish with Cape Malay heritage.

prov.popular_in: South Africa, especially Cape Town; national dish
prov.famous_for: South Africa's national dish; Cape Malay culinary heritage
prov.consists_of: Curried spiced beef mince baked with egg custard and bay leaves until golden
prov.name_origin: Derived from Indonesian 'bobotok'; brought by Cape Malay slaves in 17th century
prov.why_loved: Perfect balance of sweet and savoury; gentle spices with comforting textures
prov.method_detail: Oven bake — spiced mince topped with egg custard, baked until golden brown

brief.lead: Bobotie is South Africa's most famous traditional dish — gently spiced beef mince baked with a golden egg custard topping. The flavour profile balances mild curry spices with fruit sweetness, while the custard adds a silky texture that permeates the meat. Traditionally served with geelrys (yellow rice), this Cape Malay classic represents the beautiful fusion of European and Indonesian influences in South African cuisine.
expect: Golden-topped spiced beef with fluffy custard.
The baked custard creates a silky layer that complements the spiced meat.
Balanced sweet and savoury flavours.
Apricot chutney and raisins provide gentle sweetness against mild curry spices.
Tender mince with aromatic depth.
Slow-cooked onions and ginger-garlic base create rich foundation.
Served with yellow rice.
Turmeric-stained rice is the traditional accompaniment.

tl: Soak raisins, chop onions, brown mince in batches
Fry aromatics, add spices, incorporate chutney and mince
Transfer to dish, top with custard, bake at 180°C
Prepare and cook turmeric rice while bobotie bakes
Allow bobotie to settle, serve with yellow rice
steps: Prepare and brown the mince
Soak raisins and sultanas in warm water. Finely chop onions. Heat a large pan on high and fry mince in batches until well browned. Drain through a sieve to remove excess fat.
WHY: Browning in batches prevents the mince from stewing in its own juices, ensuring better texture and flavour.
Build the flavour base

In the same pan, fry onions with garlic-ginger paste in butter over medium-low heat until softened. Add curry powder, turmeric, and soaked raisins. Cook briefly to combine, then add tomato paste and chutney. Cook for 1 minute until tomato paste loses rawness.
WHY: Cooking the tomato paste removes its raw taste and creates depth; gentle heat prevents onions from caramelising and becoming too sweet.
Combine and season
Add ginger powder, browned mince, salt, and Worcestershire sauce. Mix well and cook on low heat for 5 minutes until everything comes together. Adjust seasoning if needed.
WHY: The final gentle cook allows flavours to meld and the mince to absorb the aromatic base.
Prepare for baking

Preheat oven to 180°C. Transfer bobotie mix to a ceramic baking dish, spreading evenly without pressing down too firmly. Whisk together eggs and milk until combined. Pour over the meat mixture. Decorate with bay leaves if desired (brush with water to prevent burning).
WHY: Not pressing the mince allows the custard to penetrate through the meat rather than sitting on top.
Bake the bobotie
Place the dish in an oven tray filled with boiling water to create a bain-marie (optional but recommended). Bake for 35-40 minutes until the custard is set and golden brown.
WHY: The bain-marie prevents edges from overcooking while the gentle heat sets the custard evenly.
Make yellow rice

While bobotie bakes, wash rice until water runs clear. Combine rice, water, salt, butter, and turmeric in a pot. Bring to boil, cover tightly, reduce to minimum heat, and cook for 15 minutes. Remove from heat and rest covered for 3 minutes.
WHY: The resting period allows rice to finish steaming through evenly for perfect texture.
Rest and serve
Remove bobotie from oven and let rest for 5-10 minutes. Serve with yellow rice and additional sides like creamed spinach or mashed potatoes.
WHY: Resting allows the custard to set slightly for cleaner serving and better texture.
pair: Traditional accompaniment with buttery turmeric flavour
Crisp white wine with good acidity balances the sweet-spiced mince

blurb: Tender beef braised slowly in Burgundy wine with pearl onions, mushrooms, and bacon — French comfort at its finest.

prov.popular_in: Burgundy region, France; worldwide French classic
prov.famous_for: Iconic French beef stew; culinary school staple; Julia Child favourite
prov.consists_of: Beef chuck braised in red wine with bacon, pearl onions, mushrooms, and herbs
prov.name_origin: Bourguignon means 'from Burgundy'; wine is essential to the dish
prov.why_loved: Deep red wine flavour with impossibly tender beef and silky vegetables
prov.method_detail: Oven braise — seared beef slow-cooked in wine until falling-apart tender

brief.lead: Beef bourguignon is France's most celebrated beef stew — chunks of beef chuck slow-braised in full-bodied red wine with bacon, pearl onions, mushrooms, and herbs. The lengthy cooking time transforms tough beef into impossibly tender meat, while the wine reduces into a velvety sauce. This is the dish that defined French country cooking and remains the ultimate expression of Burgundy's culinary heritage.
expect: Meltingly tender beef in rich wine sauce.
Hours of gentle braising break down connective tissue into silk.
Deep red wine flavour with savoury depth.
A full bottle of Burgundy wine reduces into a velvety, complex sauce.
Bacon, pearl onions, and mushrooms throughout.
Classic garnishes add sweetness and earthiness to the dish.
Served with crusty bread or potatoes.
The rich sauce needs something to soak it up.
kit: Dutch oven or heavy oven-safe pot

ing: boneless beef chuck roast (approx. 1.4kg), cut into 5cm cubes
tl: Render bacon fat, prepare vegetables and beef
Brown beef in bacon fat, set aside with bacon
Cook vegetables, tomato paste, and wine
Combine everything, cover and transfer to oven
Sear pearl onions and mushrooms, add to pot
Finish cooking, season, and rest before serving
steps: Prepare and render bacon

Preheat oven to 175°C. Dice onion, cut carrot into rounds, mince garlic. Cut bacon into 1cm pieces. Place in Dutch oven over medium heat and cook, stirring occasionally, until fat is rendered and bacon is crisp (10-12 minutes).
WHY: Rendering bacon slowly provides the fat needed for searing the beef and adds smoky depth to the dish.
Sear the beef

While bacon renders, trim excess fat from beef chuck and cut into 5cm cubes. Pat dry and season with 2 teaspoons salt and ½ teaspoon pepper. Remove bacon with slotted spoon and set aside. Add half the beef to pot in a single layer. Cook until browned on all sides (2-3 minutes per side). Transfer to bowl with bacon. Repeat with remaining beef.
WHY: Searing in batches prevents overcrowding, ensuring proper browning for flavour development.
Build the flavour base

Add onion and carrot to pot. Cook, stirring occasionally, until onion is softened (about 3 minutes). Add garlic and cook until fragrant (30 seconds). Add tomato paste and stir to coat vegetables. Cook until darkened (1-2 minutes). Sprinkle with flour and stir to coat. Cook for 1 minute to remove floury taste.
WHY: Cooking tomato paste concentrates its flavour and creates depth; flour provides gentle thickening for the sauce.
Deglaze and assemble

Add entire bottle of dry red wine to pot. Scrape up browned bits from bottom. Add beef broth, thyme sprigs, bay leaves, and remaining ½ teaspoon salt. Return beef, bacon, and any juices to pot. Increase heat to medium-high and bring to simmer.
WHY: Wine deglazes the pot, incorporating all the flavourful browned bits into the sauce.
Oven braise
Cover pot and transfer to oven. Cook until beef is very tender (about 2 hours). About 30 minutes before beef is ready, trim and peel pearl onions if needed, and quarter mushrooms.
WHY: The gentle, even heat of the oven breaks down connective tissue without risk of burning.
Prepare and add vegetables

Melt butter in large skillet over medium heat. Add pearl onions, mushrooms, remaining ½ teaspoon salt, and remaining ¼ teaspoon pepper. Cook, stirring occasionally, until tender and lightly browned (12-15 minutes). Add to pot and stir to combine. Remove and discard bay leaves and thyme stems.
WHY: Searing vegetables separately ensures they're properly cooked and coloured before joining the stew.
Final seasoning and service

Taste and adjust seasoning with more salt and pepper as needed. Remove from oven and let rest for 5-10 minutes. Garnish with chopped fresh parsley if desired. Serve with crusty bread, boiled potatoes, or over mashed potatoes.
WHY: Resting allows flavours to settle and the sauce to thicken slightly for better texture.
pair: To soak up the rich, wine-infused sauce
Same wine variety used in cooking creates perfect harmony

blurb: Crispy-skinned roast duck served with thin pancakes, spring onions, and hoisin sauce — Beijing's imperial masterpiece.

prov.famous_for: Imperial Chinese dish; crispy skin prized above all; UNESCO intangible heritage
prov.consists_of: Whole duck air-dried then roasted until skin is paper-thin and crisp
prov.name_origin: Peking (old name for Beijing); imperial court dish dating to Ming Dynasty
prov.why_loved: Extraordinarily crispy skin contrasted with juicy, succulent meat
prov.method_detail: Oven roast — air-dried duck roasted at high heat then low heat for crispy skin

brief.lead: Peking duck is Beijing's most celebrated dish — a whole duck air-dried for 24 hours, then roasted until the skin becomes impossibly crisp and thin. The duck is carved tableside and served with delicate pancakes, fresh spring onions, cucumber, and sweet bean sauce. Each diner assembles their own wraps, balancing the rich duck with refreshing vegetables. This is imperial Chinese dining at its finest.
expect: Paper-thin, impossibly crispy skin.
The lengthy air-drying process allows the skin to crisp dramatically during roasting.
Juicy, succulent meat underneath.
Protected by the skin, the meat stays moist while roasting.
Interactive assembly with pancakes and vegetables.
Each person creates their own perfect balance of duck, vegetables, and sauce.
Sweet-savoury flavour profile.
Hoisin or sweet bean sauce provides traditional Chinese sweetness.

tl: Soak, hang, and begin air-drying process with maltose glaze
Hang duck for at least 24 hours (up to 5 days in winter)
Initial roast at 180°C, then finish at 120°C
Final high-heat crisping or hot oil treatment
Rest duck, carve into thin slices
Warm pancakes, prepare vegetables, assemble wraps
steps: Prepare the duck for air-drying

Choose a lean, clean duck around 2kg. Soak in water with 1 teaspoon white vinegar for 1 hour. Using kitchen twine, tie the duck from the neck for hanging. In a small bowl, mix oyster sauce, Chinese cooking wine, and five-spice powder. Spread the mixture evenly on the inside of the duck.
WHY: The vinegar soak removes any gamey flavour; the internal seasoning adds depth to the meat.
Glaze for drying

In another small bowl, mix maltose (or honey) with hot water and ½ teaspoon white vinegar. The liquid should be warm for the first brushing. Brush the mixture evenly over the duck skin. Wait 30 minutes, then brush again. Hang the duck in a cool, well-ventilated area for at least 24 hours (up to 5 days in winter).
WHY: The maltose glaze promotes crisping; air-drying removes moisture from the skin for better texture.
Prepare for roasting
Preheat oven to 180°C. Seal the bottom of the duck by tucking the neck skin under or securing with toothpicks. Place duck on a roasting rack set over a lined baking tray to catch drips.
WHY: Sealing the bottom prevents juices from escaping during roasting, keeping the meat moist.
Initial roast
Place duck in oven and roast for 20 minutes. Carefully turn duck over and roast other side for 15 minutes.
WHY: The initial high heat begins the crisping process and renders some of the fat.
Slow-roast for tenderness
Reduce oven temperature to 120°C. Continue roasting for 30 minutes (extend by 10 minutes for each additional 500g of duck weight).
WHY: The lower, gentler heat cooks the meat through without drying it out while continuing fat rendering.
Crisp the skin (choose one method)

Option 1: Heat oil until very hot. Holding duck by the neck, carefully pour hot oil over the skin. Option 2: Increase oven temperature back to 180°C and roast for 6-10 minutes, watching carefully to prevent burning.
WHY: The final high heat creates the signature paper-thin crispy skin characteristic of Peking duck.
Rest and carve
Remove duck from oven and rest for several minutes. Carve by first cutting around the breast, then dividing in half. Slice breast meat thinly with skin attached. Shred remaining meat from bones.
WHY: Resting allows juices to redistribute; slicing thinly creates the traditional presentation.
Prepare and serve

Make or warm Peking duck pancakes. Cut cucumber into thin strips. Slice spring onions into similar strips. Mix sweet bean sauce (2 tablespoons) with sesame oil (1 tablespoon). To assemble: brush sauce on pancake, add duck slices, cucumber strips, and spring onion strips. Roll up and enjoy.
WHY: The fresh vegetables provide crunch and contrast to the rich, crispy duck.
pair: Traditional vehicle for duck, vegetables, and sauce
Light lager balances the rich duck and refreshes between bites

blurb: Leg of lamb slow-roasted in parchment with garlic, lemon, and herbs until falling-off-the-bone tender.

prov.popular_in: Greek islands; mainland Greece; Easter specialty
prov.famous_for: Traditional Greek Easter dish; underground cooking origins; melting tenderness
prov.consists_of: Whole leg of lamb slow-roasted in sealed parchment with potatoes and aromatics
prov.name_origin: Greek 'kleftis' meaning thief; stolen meat cooked secretly underground
prov.why_loved: Extraordinarily tender meat with aromatic potatoes; effortless hands-off cooking
prov.method_detail: Oven slow-roast — parchment-sealed lamb braises in own juices at low temperature

brief.lead: Lamb kleftiko is Greece's most celebrated lamb dish — a whole leg of lamb slow-roasted in a sealed parchment parcel with potatoes, olives, cheese, and aromatics until the meat falls from the bone. The parchment creates a self-braising environment where the lamb cooks in its own aromatic juices. The name means 'stolen meat,' referring to Greek rebels who would secretly cook stolen lamb in underground pits to avoid detection during Ottoman occupation.
expect: Falling-off-the-bone tender lamb.
Four hours at low temperature in parchment creates extraordinary tenderness.
Aromatic potatoes with olives and cheese.
Vegetables cook in the lamb's juices, absorbing incredible flavour.
Lemon, garlic, and cinnamon perfume.
The traditional Greek marinade creates bright, aromatic depth.
Impressive presentation at table.
Opening the parchment parcel releases a cloud of aromatic steam.

ing: fresh herb leaves (oregano, rosemary, or thyme)
Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into chunks
tl: Prepare marinade, score and coat lamb, refrigerate overnight
Cut vegetables, arrange in parchment with lamb
Fold parchment, roast at 160°C for 3.5 hours
Open parchment, roast 30 minutes more for colour
steps: Prepare the marinade
In a food processor, pulse garlic cloves, 1 teaspoon sea salt, fresh herbs, lemon zest, lemon juice, cinnamon, black pepper, and 2-3 tablespoons olive oil until combined into a paste.
WHY: Creating a marinade paste ensures even coverage and better penetration into the meat.
Marinate the lamb

Using a sharp knife, create many small holes all over the lamb. Rub the marinade paste over the lamb, pushing it into the holes. Transfer to a large pan or ziplock bag. Marinate overnight or at least 3-4 hours.
WHY: Scoring allows the marinade to penetrate deep into the meat for maximum flavour.
Prepare the parchment parcel

Preheat oven to 160°C. Cut potatoes into chunks. Arrange two long pieces of parchment paper in a large roasting pan in a cross pattern. Place lamb in the center. Surround with potatoes. Add tomatoes, cheese, and olives. Tuck fresh herb sprigs around. Drizzle with lemon juice, remaining olive oil, dried oregano, salt, and pepper.
WHY: The parchment cross ensures enough paper to completely seal the parcel for proper braising.
Seal and begin roasting
Fold parchment paper over lamb and ingredients. Tuck edges tightly to seal completely. Roast for 3.5 hours.
WHY: A tight seal creates a self-braising environment where the lamb cooks in its own aromatic juices.
Open and finish
Carefully open the parchment parcel. Return to oven for 30 minutes to brown the lamb and caramelize the vegetables.
WHY: Opening the parchment allows the exterior to brown while the interior remains moist.
Rest and serve
Remove from oven and let rest for 30 minutes. Transfer to a serving platter with vegetables. Serve warm.
WHY: Resting allows juices to redistribute for more tender, juicy meat.
pair: Crisp, acidic salad balances the rich, slow-roasted lamb
Medium-bodied Greek red with dark fruit complements lamb

blurb: Fresh crayfish grilled over coals with garlicky butter baste — South Africa's luxury braai centerpiece.

prov.popular_in: South Africa coastal areas; special occasions
prov.famous_for: South African braai luxury; sustainable seafood; simple perfection
prov.consists_of: Whole crayfish grilled over coals with garlic-chilli butter baste
prov.name_origin: South Africa; 'crayfish' refers to rock lobster (spiny lobster)
prov.why_loved: Sweet, succulent meat enhanced by smoky grilling and aromatic garlic butter
prov.method_detail: Braai — crayfish boiled then grilled over coals with garlic butter baste

brief.lead: Grilled crayfish with garlic butter is South Africa's ultimate braai luxury — fresh crayfish lightly boiled, then grilled over hot coals while being basted with garlicky butter. The crayfish's sweet, delicate meat needs nothing more than simple preparation, making it perfect for the braai. The smoke from the butter dripping onto the coals infuses the crayfish with subtle flavour that defines authentic South African coastal cooking.
expect: Sweet, succulent crayfish meat.
Fresh crayfish has naturally sweet, delicate flesh that needs minimal enhancement.
Smoky aroma from coals.
Butter dripping onto coals creates fragrant smoke that flavours the crayfish.
Garlic and chilli butter baste.
Simple, aromatic butter enhances without overwhelming the delicate seafood.
Hands-on, social eating.
Eating crayfish with your fingers is part of the experience.

tl: Bring water to boil, add crayfish, steam for 15 minutes
Ice bath, halve crayfish, remove veins, rinse
Place crayfish shell-side down, baste with garlic butter
Flip meat-side down briefly, finish cooking
steps: Prepare and boil crayfish
Break off feelers from crayfish. Add 2-3cm boiling water and pinch of salt to large pot. Add crayfish, cover, and steam on high heat for approximately 15 minutes until bright red.
WHY: Steaming in minimal water preserves the sweet flavour and prevents waterlogging.
Cool and prepare
Transfer cooked crayfish to cold water to stop cooking. Using sharp kitchen shears, cut crayfish in half lengthwise. Remove and discard the digestive tract (vein). Rinse interior clean.
WHY: Removing the vein prevents any bitterness and ensures clean, sweet flavour.
Prepare garlic butter baste
In a small saucepan over medium heat, melt butter with minced garlic, chilli flakes, and black pepper. Simmer for 2-3 minutes, then reduce to low heat for at least 10 minutes to infuse flavours.
WHY: Simmering garlic removes its raw harshness and mellows the flavour.
Prepare the braai
Prepare nice hot coals for the braai. The fire should be medium-hot — you want steady heat, not flames.
WHY: Steady, even coals provide consistent cooking without scorching the delicate meat.
Grill shell-side down
Place crayfish halves on braai grid, shell side down. Generously baste with garlic butter mixture. Cook until just warmed through and beginning to brown, basting frequently.
WHY: Shell-side down protects the delicate meat while the shell helps retain the butter baste.
Turn and finish
Turn crayfish over, meat side down, for approximately 30 seconds. The butter will drip onto coals, creating smoke that flavours the crayfish.
WHY: The brief meat-side down cooking adds smoky flavour while keeping the meat moist.
Serve immediately
Serve hot with mayonnaise and lemon wedges. Eat with fingers — this is hands-on dining.
WHY: Crayfish is best enjoyed immediately while still hot and the butter is fresh.
pair: Acidic lemon and creamy mayonnaise balance the rich butter
Crisp white wine with good acidity complements sweet crayfish

blurb: Veal shanks slowly braised in white wine until falling-off-the-bone, finished with bright gremolata.

prov.popular_in: Milan, Italy; Northern Italy; Italian restaurants worldwide
prov.famous_for: Milan's signature dish; traditionally served with risotto alla milanese
prov.consists_of: Cross-cut veal shanks braised in white wine with vegetables, finished with gremolata
prov.name_origin: Italian for 'bone with a hole'; refers to the marrow bone visible in the cross-section
prov.why_loved: Uniquely tender veal with rich marrow, brightened by fresh gremolata
prov.method_detail: Stovetop braise — veal shanks slowly cooked in wine and broth until tender

brief.lead: Osso buco is Milan's most celebrated dish — cross-cut veal shanks slowly braised in white wine and broth with vegetables until the meat falls from the bone. The marrow in the centre of each shank melts into the sauce, creating incredible richness. The dish is finished with gremolata — a bright mixture of lemon zest, parsley, and garlic sprinkled over just before serving. This classic of Lombard cuisine represents elegant comfort food at its finest.
expect: Falling-off-the-bone tender veal.
Slow braising breaks down connective tissue into meltingly tender meat.
Rich, marrow-infused sauce.
The marrow from the bones melts into the cooking liquid for extraordinary depth.
Bright gremolata finish.
Fresh lemon, parsley, and garlic cut through the richness.
Served with risotto or polenta.
Creamy Italian starches are the traditional accompaniment.

tl: Finely chop mirepoix vegetables, brown in butter
Lightly flour and brown veal in same pan
Add wine, reduce, then add broth and cover
Simmer gently for 1.5 hours until very tender
Prepare gremolata, sprinkle over, serve immediately
steps: Prepare and brown vegetables

Finely chop onion, carrot, and celery (mirepoix). In a Dutch oven or heavy pan, melt ¼ stick butter over medium heat. Add vegetables and brown until lightly coloured (about 10 minutes). Remove vegetables and set aside.
WHY: Browning vegetables develops sweetness and depth that forms the flavour foundation of the dish.
Prepare and sear veal shanks
Lightly flour veal shanks and season with salt. Return pan to medium-high heat. Add veal shanks and brown on both sides (2-3 minutes per side). Take care not to burn the vegetables remaining in pan.
WHY: Flour helps with browning and provides gentle thickening for the sauce.
Add wine and reduce
Add white wine to pan. Let it evaporate for 7-8 minutes until reduced by about half. Add pepper and beef broth.
WHY: Reducing wine concentrates its flavour and removes alcohol for sweeter, more aromatic sauce.
Braise until tender
Lower heat, cover pan, and cook for about 1.5 hours. Turn meat occasionally. If necessary, add more broth to keep meat moist. Cook until veal is tender enough to fall off the bone.
WHY: Gentle braising breaks down collagen and connective tissue into gelatin for melting texture.
Make gremolata
While veal braises, combine minced garlic, chopped parsley, and grated lemon zest (yellow part only). Mix well with mortar and pestle or press with spoon to release oils.
WHY: The mortar releases essential oils from garlic and lemon for brighter flavour.
Finish and serve
When meat is finished cooking, ensure gremolata is well mixed. Sprinkle over cooked veal. Let meat and gremolata blend for one more minute. Serve warm, traditionally with risotto alla milanese.
WHY: The brief blending allows gremolata to release oils without losing its bright, fresh character.
pair: Traditional accompaniment; creamy rice balances the rich veal
Powerful Nebbiolo-based wine stands up to the rich, marrow-infused sauce

blurb: Saffron-infused rice loaded with chicken, chorizo, and seafood — Spain's most famous festive dish.

prov.popular_in: Valencia region, Spain; coastal Spain; worldwide Spanish restaurants
prov.famous_for: Spain's national dish; festive celebration food; socarrat (crispy bottom)
prov.consists_of: Short-grain rice cooked with saffron, chicken, chorizo, seafood in wide shallow pan
prov.name_origin: Valencia, Spain; from Arabic 'baqiya' meaning leftovers
prov.why_loved: Incredible saffron flavour with varied proteins; sociable, festive presentation
prov.method_detail: Stovetop simmer — rice cooked uncovered without stirring until tender with socarrat

brief.lead: Paella is Spain's most celebrated dish — short-grain rice cooked in a wide shallow pan with saffron, chicken, chorizo, and seafood. The rice absorbs incredible flavour from the proteins and forms a crispy bottom layer called socarrat. Paella mixta (mixed paella) combines meat and seafood, though purists note this is a modern variation. This is festive, communal dining meant to be relaxed and fun — perfect for bringing people together around the pan.
expect: Tender, saffron-stained rice grains.
Bomba rice absorbs liquid without becoming mushy, maintaining distinct grains.
Crispy socarrat bottom layer.
The prized crispy bottom forms from rice cooked without stirring.
Variety of proteins throughout.
Chicken, chorizo, prawns, and mussels create diverse flavours and textures.
No-stirring cooking method.
Unlike risotto, paella is cooked uncovered without stirring for proper texture.

ing: chicken thigh fillets, cut into 3.5cm pieces
tl: Cut all ingredients, sear chorizo and squid
Sauté aromatics, brown chicken, add rice and liquids
Cook rice uncovered for 10 minutes without stirring
Squidge in prawns and mussels, cook 8 minutes more
Cover and rest 5 minutes, then serve with lemon
steps: Prepare ingredients and sear chorizo

Slice chorizo ½cm thick. Cut squid into 7mm rings. Dice onion, mince garlic, dice capsicum. Cut chicken into 3.5cm pieces. Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a 30cm paella pan or large skillet over high heat. Add chorizo and cook until browned on each side (about 3 minutes). Remove with slotted spoon and set aside.
WHY: Browning chorizo first renders its flavorful fat which will be used to cook other ingredients.
Sear squid quickly
Add squid rings to hot pan. Cook for 45 seconds on each side, then remove and set aside.
WHY: Squid cooks very quickly and becomes rubbery if overcooked — just 90 seconds total is perfect.
Build flavour base
Add remaining oil if needed. Add garlic and onion, cook for 2 minutes. Add capsicum, cook for 1 minute. Add chicken pieces. Cook for 2 minutes until lightly browned but still raw inside.
WHY: Building layers of flavour gradually creates the aromatic base that makes paella exceptional.
Add rice and liquids
Add paella rice and mix until grains are coated in oil. Add most of the chorizo (reserve ¼ for garnish), chicken stock, tomato, and saffron. Stir to combine.
WHY: Coating rice in oil helps grains remain separate during cooking for perfect texture.
Initial rice simmer

Bring to a simmer, then adjust heat to maintain gentle simmer (not rapid). Simmer for 10 minutes — no stirring. At this stage, some rice should be visible through the liquid but there should still be plenty of liquid.
WHY: No stirring is crucial — unlike risotto, paella should not be creamy; we want distinct grains.
Add seafood and finish

Scatter frozen peas over rice. Squidge prawns and mussels into rice (partial submerge is fine). Cook for 8 minutes or until prawns are opaque, mussels are open, and most liquid is absorbed. Discard any mussels that do not open.
WHY: Adding seafood partway through prevents overcooking while their juices flavour the rice.
Check and rest

Taste test rice — it should be firm but cooked through. If too firm, add splash of hot water and continue cooking. Remove from stove. Scatter over squid and reserved chorizo. Cover with lid and rest for 5 minutes.
WHY: Resting allows rice to absorb remaining liquid for perfect texture — juicy, not stodgy.
Serve
Garnish with chopped fresh parsley and lemon wedges. Take to table as is, then mix up rice with seafood before serving. The mix at the table is part of the paella ritual.
WHY: Mixing at the table distributes flavours and allows guests to customize their portions.
pair: Crisp Spanish white with good acidity complements saffron and seafood
Refreshing cold soup provides light start before rich paella
